nativeRange.habitat: Found in marine intertidal and subtidal zones worldwide, typically growing on rocky substrates within the photic zone — the depth range reached by enough sunlight to support algal growth.

avoidWith: herbivorous fish that consume macroalgae (e.g., tangs, urchins in overstocked tanks)

imageCandidates.notes: Jamie O'Neill; 2048x1536; Gelidium spinosum, sharp live in-situ tidepool specimen showing vibrant reddish-purple serrated pinnate fronds (diagnostic form)
Vardhan Patankar; 1764x1936; Gelidium sp. striking vibrant red palm-like clump growing in situ on reef; mild underwater haze so not the recommended pick
DISCO; 768x1024; Gelidium robustum, vibrant red bushy clump in tidepool beside kelp, in-water
Joxemai; 4288x3216; Gelidium sesquipedale (major agar species) showing multiple reddish-purple palm-like clumps; on wet sand rather than in water
TonyT; 1536x2048; Gelidium purpurascens, sharp deep-red cluster; species name matches the reddish-purple coloration

summary: Purple Palms is a reddish-purple display macroalgae (Gelidium sp.) that forms dense, palm-like clumps of upright strand growth. A taller-growing form known as Jumbo Gelidium is also sold. The Gelidium genus is one of the principal commercial sources of agar worldwide.
careNotes:
Purple Palms belongs to Gelidium sp., a red alga in the family Gelidiaceae (order Gelidiales, division Rhodophyta). A taller-growing form sold as Jumbo Gelidium represents the same genus. Both display a reddish-purple coloration with strand-like growth similar to Chondria, forming thick clumps that tend to vary in shape depending on flow, lighting, and other environmental conditions.

Gelidium is the major commercial agarophyte genus worldwide — Morocco, China, Korea, and Portugal are among the leading harvest producers. In the aquarium, it is kept purely as an ornamental display specimen.

Gelidium is available from a small number of specialty macroalgae retailers; it is not commonly stocked by mainstream marine livestock suppliers. Standard reef parameters are generally advised: temperature around 22–26°C, salinity SG 1.023–1.026, pH 8.0–8.3. Avoid burying the base in sand, which can cause the holdfast to melt; attachment to rockwork or rubble is recommended. Full-spectrum lighting at moderate intensity has been found suitable. Growth tends to be slow relative to green macroalgae like Chaetomorpha. Iron and iodine supplementation is sometimes reported to benefit red macros. Compatible with most reef livestock, though heavy herbivores may graze on it.
breedingNotes: In the wild, Gelidium species reproduce through an alternation of generations — a two-stage life cycle in which a sexual phase (gametophyte) alternates with an asexual spore-producing phase (tetrasporophyte). In the aquarium, propagation is typically achieved by simple fragmentation: detaching a cutting and reattaching it to a new piece of rock or rubble.
